## 1. Chemical Reactions and Equations
## CHEMICAL EQUATION
An atom of an element is represented by symbol, the molecule of a compound by a formula, and a chemical change is represented by an equation. Thus, the symbolic representation of an actual chemical change is a chemical equation.
## To write an equation -
(a) Substances which take part in chemical reaction (i.e., reactants) are usually written on left hand side and substances formed as a result of chemical reaction (i.e., products) are written on right hand side. The reactants and products are separated by an arrow head mark ( → ) which starts from reactants and points towards the products. Sign of equal to (=) may also be used in place of arrow head mark.
(b) Each reactant or the product is separated from one another by using plus sign ( + ) in between them.
The chemical equation written, following the above two steps, is skeletal chemical equation, as it does not have same number of atoms of each element on both sides of the arrow i.e. unbalanced chemical equation.
## BALANCED CHEMICAL EQUATION
A chemical equation in which number of atoms of each element is equal, both in products and reactants, is a balanced chemical equation.
## Example :
(i) Magnesium burns with oxygen and forms magnesium oxide.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s}) \\
& 2 \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})
\end{aligned}
$$
(Skeletonequation)
(Balanced equation)
Note: Magnesium burns in air with a brilliant white light, and for this reason it is often used in flares and fireworks.
(ii) Magnesium also burns in an environment of carbon dioxide such as in a beaker full of dry ice.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{~s}) \\
& 2 \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{~s})
\end{aligned}
$$
(Skeletonequation)
(Balancedequation)
(Skeletonequation)
(Balancedequation)
Note: When the Haber synthesis is carried out industrially, immense numbers of molecules are involved, but for every one molecule of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ that reacts with three molecules of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$, two molecules of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ are produced.
## Features of the Balanced Equation \section*{Equation}
(a) The reactants appear on the left, and products appear on the right. The arrow joining them indicates the direction of reaction.
(b) An integer preceedes the formula of each molecule. These integers are known as stoichiometric coefficients. The quantitative relation among reactants and products is known as stoichiometry. The stoichiometric coefficient for $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ in the equation (iii) is 3 and that of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is 2 . When no number appears, we understand that the stoichiometric coefficient is 1 . The stoichiometric coefficient for $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ in this equation is 1 .
(c) The stoichiometric coefficients in a chemical equation are the set of smallest integers that gives a balanced equation. It refers to the relative number of molecules involved in the reaction.
(d) Charge is conserved in balanced equation. In this equation, all participants are neutral species, so charge is conserved regardless of the stoichiometric coefficients. For reactions that include ions, however, charge conservation must be explicitly addressed.
- The amount of product actually obtained in a reaction is called actual yield.

## BALANCING OF A CHEMICAL EQUATION
According to law of conservation of mass, "Mass can neither be created nor destroyed"
In a chemical reaction total mass of the reactant should be equal to total mass of the product. If not, then we need to balance chemical equation. Let us understand the balancing of a chemical equation :
There is no definite rule for balancing an equation. Therefore, it requires a good deal of skill and practice. The equation is balanced by a method called hit and trial method.
## Hit and Trial Method
Lets consider word equation of a reaction between nitrogen and hydrogen producing ammonia.
Nitrogen + Hydrogen ⟶ Ammonia
Steps involved in balancing the above equation are as following :
## Step-1
First of all write the skeletal equation for the reaction and count the number of atoms of each substance on either side of the equation.
| $\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ | | |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Element | Number of atoms in reactants | Number of atoms in product |
| N | 2 | 1 |
| H | 2 | 3 |
(Skeletalequation)
## Step-2
Balance the atom which is minimum in number, on either side of chemical equation. In the given equation, number of nitrogen is the minimum on the RHS.
To balance the nitrogen atom, multiply the nitrogen in RHS by 2, so that the number of nitrogen atoms becomes equal on both sides.
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \quad \text { (partly balanced equation) }
$$
It should be noted that to equalise the number of atoms on both sides, we cannot alter the formulae of the compounds or elements involved in the reaction.
## Step-3
Still number of H atoms is not balanced. To equalise the number of H atoms, multiply the hydrogen molecule $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)$ on LHS by 3 .
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}
$$
## Step-4
Now examine the number of atoms of N and H in the new chemical equation.
| Elements | Number of atoms in reactants | Number of atoms in product |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| N | 2 | 2 |
| H | 6 | 6 |
The number of atoms of each element are equal on both sides. Thus, this equation is now balanced.
## Step-5
The above balanced equation does not give any information about the physical state of reactants and products.
In order to make the equation more informative, the physical states of reactants and products are mentioned along with their chemical formulae. These physical states are mentioned with the help of certain notations. These notations are -
(g) for gases, (l) for liquids,(s) for solid, (aq) for aqueous; The word aqueous (aq) is written if the reactant or product is present as a solution in water.
The balanced equation becomes
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
## Example :
Hydrogen reacts with chlorine to give hydrochloric acid.
(i) The skeleton equation of the above reaction :
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HCl}
$$
(ii) To balance chlorine atoms, multiply the molecule HCl by 2 .
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$
Now, number of chlorine atoms and oxygen atoms are equal on both sides. Hence, the equation is balanced.
(iii) To make the chemical equation more informative, now write physical states of the reactants and products.
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
## Implications of a Chemical Equation
A chemical equation conveys a large number of informations as described below.
## Qualitative information
(a) It tells us about the names of the reactants and products.
(b) It tells us about the physical states of the reactants and products.
## Quantitative information
(a) It expresses the number of molecules of reactants and products.
(b) It expresses the relative number of moles of the reactants and the products.
(c) It expresses the relative weight of the reactants and the products.
(d) It expresses the volume of the gaseous reactants and products.
## Example :
Ammonia gas is formed by the combination of nitrogen and hydrogen.
$$
\begin{array}{cc}
\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\underset{3 \mathrm{~V}}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2}}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \underset{2 \mathrm{~V}}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})} \\
28 & 6
\end{array}
$$
The above equation expresses that-
(i) Nitrogen combines with hydrogen to form ammonia.
(ii) $\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, all three are in gaseous physical state.
(iii) One molecule of nitrogen combines with three molecules of hydrogen to form two molecules of ammonia.
(iv) 28 parts by weight of nitrogen has combined with 6 parts by weight of hydrogen to form 34 parts by weight of ammonia.
(v) One volume of nitrogen and three volumes of hydrogen combine together to give 2 volumes of ammonia gas.
## Limitations of a Chemical Equation
A chemical equation does not give the following informations :
(a) Conditions necessary for a chemical reaction : A chemical equation does not necessarily indicate the reaction conditions.
(b) Rate of reaction : It does not give any idea about the rate of the reaction, whether it is slow or fast.
(c) Change of heat : It fails to give any indication whether the heat is absorbed or evolved.
(d) Concentration : It does not give any indication about the concentration of the reactants.
(e) Mechanism : It fails to give any idea about the mechanism of the reaction.
(f) Nature of reaction : It fails to give any information about the nature of reaction, whether it is reversible or irreversible reaction.
## Removal of Drawbacks of a Chemical Equation
Limitations of chemical reactions have been partially removed.
(a) To express the concentration of a substance the word 'conc.' for concentrated and 'dil' for dilute are written just below the reactant or after the formula of the reactant, viz. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (conc.)
$$
\underset{\text { (conc.) }}{\mathrm{Cu}}+\underset{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}{4} \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
(b) If a substance formed in the reaction is gas, it is indicated putting an arrow pointing upwards after the formula.
$$
\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow
$$
If a substance is precipitated in the reaction, it is indicated by putting arrow pointing downwards after the formula.
$$
\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl} \downarrow+\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}
$$
(c) Reversible reaction is indicated by the sign of reversibility ( ⇌).
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO} \uparrow
$$
(d) If a reaction take place at a particular temperature or pressure, it is expressed by writing above or below the arrow head.
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow[450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}]{200 \mathrm{~atm}} 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \uparrow
$$
(e) If the reaction is exothermic the energy released is expressed by plus ( + ) sign and if the reaction is endothermic, it is expressed by putting minus (-) sign on RHS.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+94300 \mathrm{cal} \\
& \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}-83200 \mathrm{cal}
\end{aligned}
$$
## SOLUTION :
(a) 2 Na (s) $+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (l) $\longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}$ (g) +2 NaOH (aq)
(b) $2 \mathrm{KClO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(c) $\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})$
(d) $2 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Check whether the following represents the correct balanced chemical equation or not.
(a) $\quad \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CuO}+4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
Copper nitrate
## CHEMICAL REACTION
The process in which two or more substances reacts to form new substances, is called chemical reactions.
It involves making and breaking of bond between atoms to form new a substance, which describes the nature and specific characteristic of the reaction.
## Types of Chemical Reactions
Based on the specific characteristics and the nature of the reactions, chemical reactions have been divided into following types :
## 1. Synthesis or Combination Reaction
When two or more elements or substances combine together to form a new substance, the chemical reaction is known as synthesis or combination reaction. This type of reaction takes place in presence of heat, light or may require electricity.
Examples :
$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeO} \\
2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{gathered}
$$
Combination reactions have wide applications in manufacturing of some industrially important compounds.
(1) At a commercial scale, ammonia is manufactured by Haber's process.
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g)
$$
(2) Ostwald process for manufacturing of nitric acid.
$$
4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(a q)
$$
(3) Contact process for manufacturing of sulphuric acid involves multiple stages of combination reactions.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l}) \\
& \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
(\mathrm{Conc})
\end{array} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}(\mathrm{l}) \\
& \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l})
\end{aligned}
$$
(4) Manufacturing of ethyl alcohol by acid catalysed hydration of ethylene.
$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{l})
$$
## Combination reactions can occur between following types of reactants:
(a) Combination reaction between two elements.
(a) Combination between two non-metals: When a mixture of hydrogen and chlorine is placed in sunlight, hydrogen chloride gas is formed.
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Sunlight }} 2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$
(b) Combination between a metal and a non-metal : When magnesium wire is burnt in air, magnesium oxide is formed.
$$
2 \mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} 2 \mathrm{MgO}
$$
(b) Combination reaction between two compounds.
These are the reactions in which two compounds directly combine to form a new compound.
(a) Calcium oxide directly combines with water to form calcium hydroxide.
$$
\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}
$$
(b) Calcium oxide directly combines with carbon dioxide gas to form calcium carbonate.
$$
\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}
$$
(c) Formation of sulphuric acid from combination of sulphur trioxide and water which is a major contributor to nasty and toxic acid rain.
$$
\mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
(c) Combination reaction between a compound and an element.
In these reaction in which an element reacts with a compound to form a new substance.
Combination of oxygen with carbon monoxide produces carbondioxide gas.
$$
2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
## SOLUTION :
The reaction of Cs with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ are simple combination reactions between two elements, one metal and one nonmetal, forming the compound CsH and CsCl ,
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{Cs}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CsCl}(\mathrm{~s}) \\
2 \mathrm{Cs}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CsH}(\mathrm{~s})
\end{aligned}
$$
## Learn More
Reaction between calcium oxide, also known as quick lime, with water produces calcium hydroxide (slaked lime). calcium hydroxide produced in the above reaction remains as a white suspension.
$$
\underset{\text { Quick lime }}{\mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow} \underset{\text { Slaked lime }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})}
$$
It is filtered and solution containing soluble calcium hydroxide is used for white washing walls. Calcium hydroxide reacts slowly with the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ in air to form a thin layer of carbonate on the walls. Calcium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}\right)$ is formed after two to three days of white washing and gives a shiny finish to the wall.
Combination reactions involve the formation of new bonds, and this process releases a large amount of energy in the form of heat. Those reactions which occur with the evolution of heat, are known as exothermic reactions.
Example : Formation of calcium hydroxide.
Quick lime reacts with water to form slaked lime. This reaction is highly vigorous as well as exothermic in nature.
$$
\underset{\text { Quick lime }}{\mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Slaked lime }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{~s})}
$$
## 2. Decomposition Reaction
The reactions in which a substance is decomposed into two or more simpler substances, are known as decomposition reactions. Decomposition reactions involve the breaking of chemical bonds to form new substances. Hence, they require energy in the form of heat, light, electricity, etc.
Those reactions which absorbs energy in the form of heat are known as endothermic reactions.
Examples :
(i) When potassium chlorate is heated, it is decomposed into potassium chloride and oxygen.
$$
2 \mathrm{KClO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} 2 \mathrm{KCl}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}
$$
(ii) When calcium carbonate $\left(\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}\right)$ or zinc carbonate $\left(\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}\right)$ is heated CaO and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ or ZnO and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ respectively are formed.
$$
\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$
- The fermentation of grapes to make wine is also an example of decomposition reaction.
- Decomposition reactions also takes place by light.
(1) Decomposition of white silver chloride by sunlight turns it to grey.
$$
2 \mathrm{AgCl} \xrightarrow{\text { light }} 2 \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}
$$
(2) Silver bromide also decomposes by light as AgCl. This reaction is used in black \& white photography.
$$
2 \mathrm{AgBr}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\text { light }} 2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
(3) Light induced decomposition of indocyanine green (ICG). It is water-soluble tricarbo cyanine dye.
- Electrolysis of water is the decomposition of water into hydrogen and oxygen due to passage of electric current through it.
$$
2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \xrightarrow[\text { current }]{\text { Electric }} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
- Thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate into simple substance when heated e.g. decomposition of carbonate into simpler substances.
$$
\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Heat}} \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
## SOLUTION :
The description in the problem tells us what happens to the starting material: $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ breaks apart into molecules of $\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. An unbalanced form of the equation can be written from this description. Then we must balance each element in turn by inspection.
$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
## Step 1
First, count all atoms of each element on each side of the equation to see whether the elements are balanced:
$$
2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}+2 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O}
$$
## Step 2
As we can see that nitrogen is already balanced. To balance the hydrogen atoms, we change the stoichiometric coefficient of water from 1 to 2 :
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+4 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
## Step 3
This step balances nitrogen and hydrogen, but oxygen is not yet balanced. Oxygen must be balanced without changing the 1 : 1 ratio of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ to $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and the $1: 2$ ratio of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. These ratios must be retained to keep hydrogen and nitrogen in balance. Thus the only coefficient that can be adjusted to balance the oxygen atoms is that of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. Two oxygen atoms are already present as in water and thus the remaining oxygen atom can be adjusted. by changing the coefficient of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ to $1 / 2$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
This balances the equation, but it is unrealistic from a molecular perspective because there is no such thing as half a molecule. To get rid of the $1 / 2$ without unbalancing the reaction, we multiply all the coefficients by 2 .
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
4 \mathrm{~N}+8 \mathrm{H}+6 \mathrm{O} & \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{~N}+8 \mathrm{H}+6 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now the reaction is balanced and reasonable from a molecular perspective.
Copper nitrate $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]$ gives positive ring test of nitrate ion ( $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$), but after heating small amount of copper nitrate in dry boiling tube over the burner flame, it does not give positive ring test any more because it undergoes thermal decomposition reaction.
$$
2 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

## 3. Simple Displacement Reactions (Simple Substitution)
A displacement reaction occurs because a more reactive element displaces a less reactive one from a solution of its compound.
When we immerse a strip of zinc metal in a blue aqueous solution of copper (II) and remove the zinc strip it looks as if it has changed into copper. Actually, what happened is that a coating of copper is formed on the metal strip.
$\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})$
Figure : Single-replacement reaction
Some metals are so reactive that they can displace hydrogen from water, these reaction are also termed as hydrolysis reaction.
$$
2 \mathrm{~K}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
$$
## Learn More
To decide if a displacement reaction will happen or not, we must have the knowledge of activity series. In activity series the element placed above will displace the element placed below from its compound.
Now reactivity of different metals is decided on the basis of reactivity series.
Reactivity series or activity series is an empirical series of metals, in order of "reactivity" from highest to lowest. It is used to summarise the information about the reactions of metals with acids and water, single displacement reactions and the extraction of metals from their ores.
| Metal | Ion | Reactivity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| K | $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ | reacts with water |
| Na | $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ | |
| Li | $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ | |
| Sr | $\mathrm{Sr}^{2+}$ | |
| Ca | $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ | |
| Mg | $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ | reacts with acids |
| Al | $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ | |
| C | | included for comparison |
| Zn | $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ | reacts with acids |
| Cr | $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ | |
| Fe | $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ | |
| Cd | $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$ | |
| Co | $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ | |
| Ni | $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ | |
| Sn | $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$ | |
| Pb | $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ | |
| $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ | included for comparison |
| Cu | $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ | may react with some strongly oxidizing acids |
| Ag | $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ | |
| Hg | $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ | |
| Au | $\mathrm{Au}^{3+}$ | |
| Pt | $\mathrm{Pt}^{2+}$ | |
## Displacement reactions in solutions
A more reactive metal will displace a less reactive metal from its compounds in solution. These are the sorts of things you observe in this type of reaction:
- The more reactive metal gradually dissolves.
- The less reactive metal coats the more reactive metal.
- The solution may change colour.
- The tube gets warm because these reactions are exothermic.
Here are two examples of these displacement reactions.
(a) Magnesium and copper sulphate solution
Magnesium is more reactive than copper, so it displaces copper from copper sulphate solution.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Magnesium }+ \text { Copper sulphate } & \longrightarrow \text { Magnesium sulphate }+ \text { Copper } \\
\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})
$$
Displacement reaction in this reaction can be witnessed, as blue coloured solution of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ becomes colourless due to formation of $\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}$ (see figure (a) below).
Figure (a) : Displacement of copper from copper sulphate solution
Figure (b) : Iron becomes coated with copper in this displacement reaction
## (b) Iron and copper sulphate solution
Iron is more reactive than copper, so it displaces copper from copper sulphate solution.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { iron }+ \text { copper sulphate } & \longrightarrow \text { iron sulphate }+ \text { copper } \\
\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})
\end{aligned}
$$
In this reaction displacement could be witnessed, as blue coloured solution of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ changes in green colour due to formation of $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ (see figure (b) above).
At first a color change is observed when a piece of aluminium metal is dropped into green colored aqueous solution of ferrous sulphate same is the case when piece of Zinc metal is dropped. But no such color change is observed when a piece of copper metal is dropped in green coloured aqueous solution of ferrous sulphate.
## 4. Double Decomposition Reaction (Double Displacement Reaction) :
The reactions in which mutual exchange of ions takes place are known as double decomposition reactions. As a result of double decomposition reactions, two new substances are formed.
(a) Aqueous barium chloride reacts with sulfuric acid to form solid barium sulfate and hydrochloric acid.
$$
\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
(b) Sodium sulfide reacts with hydrochloric acid to form sodium chloride and hydrogen sulfide gas.
$$
\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
(c) Potassium hydroxide reacts with nitric acid to form water and potassium nitrate.
$$
\mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{KNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
## CONNECTING TOPIC
Double displacement reactions can be further classified as precipitation, gas formation, and acid-base neutralization reactions.
1. Precipitation reactions : A precipitation reaction occurs when two solutions are mixed together and a solid separates from the solution. The solid part that forms and separates from the solution is called the precipitate.
$$
\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{KI}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{PbI}_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \downarrow+2 \mathrm{KNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
2. Gas formation reaction : A double-displacement reaction in which one of the product formed is gas, is called gas formation reactions. For example, sulfide salts react with acids to form gaseous hydrogen sulfide.
$$
\mathrm{ZnS}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow
$$
All gases are soluble in water to some extent, but only a few gases [like $\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ ] are highly soluble.
3. Neutralization reactions : Neutralization reaction is a special kind of double displacement reactions. In an acidbase reaction, an acid reacts with equal quantity of a base. The acid-base reaction results in the formation of salt and water.
$$
\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$
## 5. Oxidation-Reduction Reactions
(a) Oxidation reactions :
Oxidation is defined as a process in which a substance gains oxygen or loses hydrogen during a chemical reaction.
Examples :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{S}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2} \\
& \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
Based on classical concept, oxidation reaction can also be defined as following:
(i) Removal of hydrogen : Chlorine oxidises hydrogen sulphide to sulphur by the removal of hydrogen.
$$
\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{S}
$$
(ii) Addition of oxygen : When carbon is heated in oxygen gas, carbon dioxide is obtained.
$$
\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$
(iii) Addition of more electronegative element: Chlorine is added to stannous chloride to from stannic chloride.
$$
\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{SnCl}_{4}
$$
(b) Reduction : Reduction is defined as a process which involves the loss of oxygen or gain of hydrogen during a chemical reaction.
## Examples :
$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \\
\mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{C} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{CO}}
\end{gathered}
$$
Based on classical classification reduction can also be defined as following:
(i) Removal of oxygen : When copper (II) oxide is heated in hydrogen gas, copper metal is obtained.
$$
\mathrm{CuO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
(ii) Addition of hydrogen : Combination of hydrogen and sulphur.
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{S} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}
$$
(iii) Removal of electronegative element: Chlorine is removed from ferric chloride to form ferrous chloride.
$$
2 \mathrm{FeCl}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$

Sodium metal is oxidised as it is gaining oxygen.
(b) $\mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Copper oxide is getting reduced as it is losing oxygen.

## CONCEPTS OF OXIDATION-REDUCTION REACTIONS
1. Modern Concept or Electronic Concept
(a) Oxidation : According to modern concept, the process which involves the loss of one or more electrons from an atom or ion or a molecule is called oxidation (de-electronation).
Example :
$\mathrm{Mg} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{4-} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}+\mathrm{e}^{-}$
In the above reactions, observe that species on the LHS undergoes loss of electron, therefore, they are oxidized and thus the reaction involved is oxidation reaction.
(b) Reduction : According to modern concept, the process which involves gain of one or more electrons by an atom, an ion or a molecule is called reduction (electronation).
Example :
$$
\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{Sn}^{4+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}^{2+} & ; & \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-} \\
2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} & ; & {\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{6-}} \\
\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}^{-} & ; & \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{array}
$$
In the above reaction, observe the species on the RHS which undergoes gain of electron, therefore, they are reduced; and thus the reaction involved is reduction reaction.
## 2. Oxidation Number Concept
(a) Oxidation : According to this concept, increase in oxidation number of an element in a reaction is called oxidation.
(b) Reduction : According to this concept, decrease in oxidation number of an element in a reaction is called reduction.
Example :
Thus, here $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ is oxidised to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ while $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{Mn}^{7+}$ is reduced to $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$.
## OXIDATION NUMBER
It is the number of electrons lost or gained by an element during a chemical change in a particular compound.
## Or
It is defined as the formal charge present on an atom in a particular compound determined by certain arbitrary rules.
## Rules for Determining Oxidation Number
(i) O.N. of elements in free state or uncombined state is zero. $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}, \mathrm{~N}_{2}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Ca}$ etc has zero oxidation state.
(ii) O.N. of hydrogen is always +1 except in ionic metal hydrides where it is -1 .
(iii) O.N. of oxygen is -2 except in $\mathrm{OF}_{2}$ where it is +2 and in peroxides where it is -1 .
(iv) O.N. of metals is always + ve. For IA group elements it is +1 and for IIA group elements it is +2.
(v) O.N. of halogens is -1 in metal halides.
(vi) O.N. of ion or radical is the number of electrons it must gain or lose to acquire neutrality i.e. it is equal to the electric charge, e.g. for $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ O.N., is -2 .
(vii) O.N. of an atom within compound can be $+\mathrm{ve},-\mathrm{ve}$ integer, zero or fraction.
(viii) The algebric sum of all the O.N. of elements in a compound is equal to zero.
(ix) The algebric sum of all the O.N. of elements in an ion is equal to net charge on the ion.
(x) Maximum O.N. of an element is equal to number of valence electrons i.e. group number.
(xi) Minimum O.N. of an element (except metals) $=$ (Group number -8 ).
A compound can undergo both oxidation as well as reduction, if the O.N. of one of its element is in between the maximum and the minimum value.
## For example,
Maximum O.N. of N is +5 and minimum O.N. of N is -3 .
O.N. of N is $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$ is +3 . $\mathrm{So}, \mathrm{HNO}_{2}$ can undergo oxidation and reduction both, since $\mathrm{O} . \mathrm{N}$. of N is between maximum and minimum value.
## Calculation/ Determination of Oxidation Number of Underlined Element in Some Compounds
## (a) $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$
Let the O.N. of Cr be $x$ then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \times(+1)+2 \times(x)+7 \times(-2)=0 \\
& 2+2 x-14=0, \quad \therefore x=+6
\end{aligned}
$$
## (c) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
Let the O.N. of S be $x$ then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \times(+1)+1 \times(x)+4 \times(-2)=0 \\
& 2+x-8=0, \quad \therefore \quad x=+6
\end{aligned}
$$
(b) $\quad \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$
Let the O.N. of Mn be $x$ then
$1 \times(+1)+1 \times(x)+4 \times(-2)=0$
$1+x-8=0, \quad \therefore x=+7$
(d) $\quad \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$
Let the O . N. of P be $x$ then
$1 \times(x)+4 \times(-2)=-3$
$x-8=-3, \quad \therefore x=+5$
## REDOX REACTIONS
Those reactions in which oxidation and reduction takes place simultaneously, are known as redox reactions.
## Example :
$$
\mathrm{PbO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~Pb}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
During this reaction lead oxide is losing oxygen and thus, being reduced. The hydrogen is gaining oxygen and thus being oxidised. Hence we can say that in a redox (oxidation-reduction) reaction one substance gets oxidised while the other one gets reduced.

## Oxidizing Agent
- The substance which gives oxygen for oxidation is called an oxidizing agent.
- The substance which removes hydrogen is also called an oxidizing agent.
- The substance which undergoes gain of electron and oxidizes the other substance is called an oxidizing agent.
## Reducing Agent
- The substance which gives hydrogen for reduction is called a reducing agent.
- The substance which removes oxygen is also called a reducing agent.
- The substance which undergoes loss of electron and reduces the other substance is called a reducing agent.
Here CuO oxidises $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ reduces CuO therefore in this reaction CuO is a oxidising agent whereas $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is a reducing agent.

## REDOX REACTIONS
## (A) Oxidation Number Method
In a balanced redox reaction, total increase in oxidation number must be equal to the total decrease in oxidation number. This equivalence provides the basis for balancing redox reactions.
The general procedure involves the following steps :
(a) Select the atom in oxidising agent whose oxidation number decreases and indicate the gain of electrons.
(b) Select the atom in reducing agent whose oxidation number increases and indicate the loss of electrons.
(c) Now cross multiply i.e. multiply oxidising agent by the number of lost electrons and reducing agent by number gained electrons.
(d) Balance the number of atoms on both sides whose oxidation number changes in the reaction.
(e) In order to balance oxygen atoms, add $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecules to the side deficient in oxygen. Then, balance the number of H atoms by adding $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions to the side deficient in hydrogen.
(f) If the reaction occurs in basic medium, then add $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions, equal to the number of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions, on both sides of the equation.
## EXAMPLE : Balance the following redox reaction by oxidation number method.
$$
\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+\mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
Sol. Writing the oxidation number of all the atoms.
$$
\stackrel{0+1+5-2}{\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{HNO}_{3}} \longrightarrow \stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{Cu}}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+5-2+\mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
There is change in oxidation number of Cu and N .
$\stackrel{0}{\mathrm{Cu}} \longrightarrow \stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{Cu}}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
(Oxidation no. of Cu increases by 2 )
$$
\stackrel{+5}{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}} \longrightarrow \stackrel{+4}{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}
$$
(Oxidation no. of N decreases by 1 )
To make the increase and decrease of electrons equal, eq. (2) is multiplied by 2 .
$\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
Balancing nitrate ions, hydrogen and oxygen, the following equation is obtained.
$\mathrm{Cu}+4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
This is the balanced equation.
## (B) Ion Electron Method (Half Reaction Method)
The following steps are followed while balancing redox reactions (equations) by this method.
(a) Write the equation in ionic form.
(b) Split the redox equation into two half reactions, one representing oxidation half reaction and the other reduction half reaction.
(c) Balance these half reactions separately and then add by multiplying with suitable coefficients so that the electrons are cancelled.
Balancing is done as follows:
(i) Write both half reactions and then balance the atoms other than ' O ' and ' H '.
(ii) Balance oxygen atoms by adding $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecules to the side deficient in oxygen. The number of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecules added is equal to the deficiency of oxygen atoms.
(iii) Balance hydrogen atoms by adding $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions equal to the deficiency, on the side which is deficient in hydrogen atoms.
(iv) If the medium of reaction is basic, $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions equal in number of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$are added to both sides of balanced equation.
(v) Balance the charge by adding electrons to the side which is rich in + ve charges. i.e. deficient in electrons. Number of electrons added is equal to the deficiency.
(vi) Multiply the half equations with suitable coefficients to equalize the number of electrons.
(vii) Add these half equations to get an equation which is balanced with respect to charge and atoms.
EXAMPLE: Balance the following reactions by ion electron method.
(a) $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{OH}^{-}} \mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}$
## Sol. (a) Acidic Medium :
(i) Given equation
$$
\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$
(ii) Write both the half reactions.
$\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ (Reduction half reaction)
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ (Oxidation half reaction)
(iii) Atoms other than H and O are balanced
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+} \\
& \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
(iv) Balance O atoms by the addition of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ to another side
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
& \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
(v) Balance H atoms by the addition of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions to another side.
$$
\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC
(vi) Now, balance the charge by the addition of electrons (e).
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
& \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$
(vii) Multiply equations by a constant to get number of electrons same on both side. In the above case second equation is multiplied by 3 and then added to first equation.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
& \frac{3 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-}}{\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+3 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}
\end{aligned}
$$
## (b) Alkaline medium :
(i) Given equation
$$
\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{OH}^{-}} \mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}
$$
(ii) Separate the two half reactions.
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-} & \begin{array}{l}
\text { (Oxidation half reaction) } \\
\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}^{-}
\end{array} \\
\text {(Reduction half reaction) }
\end{array}
$$
(iii) Balance O atoms by adding $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}$
$\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(iv) Balance H atoms by adding $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ to side having deficiency of H atoms and an equal no. of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions to the other side ( ∵ medium is alkaline)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 5 \mathrm{OH}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}+5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
& 5 \mathrm{OH}^{-}+\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
& \mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \text {or } \\
& \mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}^{-}+6 \mathrm{OH}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$
(v) Balance the charges by electrons
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 5 \mathrm{OH}^{-}+\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{OH}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$
(vi) Multiply first equation by 2 and add to second to give
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 10 \mathrm{OH}^{-}+2 \mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \\
& \hline 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}+2 \mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-} \longrightarrow 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$
## EXOTHERMIC AND ENDOTHERMIC REACTION
Chemical reactions usually proceed with either liberation of heat or the absorption of heat. The constituent elements of a compound are bound by a chemical bond, during the chemical reaction or chemical change these bonds between the constituents of the reactants are broken and the products are formed having new bonds. Breaking of a chemical bond releases energy while formation of a chemical bond requires energy.
Therefore, when a chemical reaction liberates heat to the surroundings, it is said to be 'exothermic reaction' and when it absorbs the heat, it is said to be 'endothermic reaction'.
## Examples :
(i) Nitrogen and hydrogen combine together to form ammonia. This chemical reaction liberates heat to the surroundings and, therefore, it is exothermic reaction.
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+22.4 \mathrm{kcal} .
$$
Here bond between two atoms of a nitrogen molecule and between two atoms of the three hydrogen molecules are broken and new bonds between nitrogen and hydrogen atoms are formed. Here the sum of energies needed to break nitrogennitrogen bond and hydrogen-hydrogen bond is less than the energy released during the formation of the bonds between nitrogen and hydrogen. Thus the heat (energy) is liberated to the surroundings. Therefore, this reaction is exothermic. Similarly the formation of sulphur trioxide from sulphur dioxide and oxygen is also exothermic reaction :
$$
2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}+42.00 \mathrm{kcal} .
$$
(ii) Nitrogen and oxygen combine together to form nitric oxide. Here the heat from the surroundings is absorbed. Therefore, this reaction is endothermic.
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}-43.2 \mathrm{kcal} \text {. }
$$
The sum of energies needed to break nitrogen-nitrogen and oxygen-oxygen bonds is much more than the energy released during the formation of the bonds between nitrogen and oxygen atoms. Therefore, heat energy is provided externally to complete the reaction. Thus this is reaction is endothermic.

## CORROSION (EROSION BY CHEMICAL ACTION)
For most of us, corrosion is the degradation of metals and is often called rust.Technically we can define it as an irreversible interfacial reaction of a material (metal, ceramic, polymer) with its environment which results in consumption of the material. Corrosion causes damage to car bodies, bridges, iron railings, ships and to all objects made of metals, specially those of iron. Corrosion of iron (rusting) is a serious problem. Every year an enormous amount of money is spent to replace damaged iron. Most metals corrode on contact with water (and moisture in the air), acids, bases, salts, oils, aggressive metal polishes, and other solid and liquid chemicals. Metals will also corrode when exposed to gaseous materials like acid vapors, formaldehyde gas, ammonia gas, and sulfur containing gases. Corrosion specifically refers to any process involving the deterioration or degradation of metal components. The best known case is that of the rusting of iron. Corrosion processes are usually electrochemical in nature, having the essential features of a battery.
When corrosion is localised and create small holes or pits in the metal, it is called pitting
Did You Know? corrosion. Pitting corrosion occurs much faster in areas where microstructural changes have occurred due to welding operations.
## Factors Influencing Corrosion
Following factors influence the corrosion -
(i) Reactivity of metals
(ii) Presence of moisture and atmospheric gases like $\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{SO}_{2}$ etc
(iii) Presence of impurities
(iv) Strains in the metal
(v) Presence of electrolyte

## SOLUTION :
The unbalanced equation is :
$$
\mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
We see that both Al and O appear only once on each side of the equation but in unequal numbers. To balance the Al atoms, we place 2 in front of Al :
$$
2 \mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
There are two O atoms on the left-hand side and three O atoms on the right-hand side of the equation. This inequality of O atoms can be eliminated by writing $\frac{3}{2}$ in front of the $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ on the left-hand side of the equation.
$$
2 \mathrm{Al}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
Now multiply the entire equation by 2 to convert $3 / 2$ to 3 .
$$
\text { i.e. } 4 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
The above equation is the balanced equation for the formation of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.
Place a small silver article (made up of pure silver) in open air for few days. Then record your observation. In case if there is any sort of change, then try to find out the exact reason for this change by discussing with your friends and teacher.
## RANCIDITY
The most important cause of deterioration in fats and fatty foods is oxidation. Oxidation of fats results in the replacement of an oxygen ion from a hydrogen ion in the fatty acid molecule. This substitution destabilizes the molecule and makes it possible for other odd chemical fragments to find a place along the chain. An unpleasant change in the flavor and odour of a food is develop, called rancidity. Unsaturated fats are more susceptible to oxidation than the saturated fats.
Factors which accelerate fat oxidation include trace metals (iron, zinc, etc.), salt, light, water, bacteria, and molds. Fat oxidation can be retarded by use of antioxidants (Antioxidants are compounds that inhibit chemical reactions with oxygen such as BHT, BHA, vitamin E, and vitamin C), by use of spices such as sage and rosemary, and by use of light and/or air tight wrapping.
Some high fat foods such as potato chips are packed in materials that protect them from light and oxygen and the containers are flooded with nitrogen to further exclude oxygen. The nitrogen also serves as a cushion to minimize breakage of the chips during transport.
## Learn
## More
Oxidation reactions may involve highly reactive molecules called free radicals. Free radicals are molecules that have lost an electron and try to gain it by reacting with other molecules. This causes the substance to break down. Metals often catalyze reactions with oxygen. Another example would be the half eaten apple. The skinless area quickly oxidized causing the apple to turn brown. Antioxidants inhibit these changes by reacting with the free radicals before they can react with oxygen (free radical scavenging) or by reacting with the metals. Naturally occurring antioxidants may also work by interacting directly with oxygen.
Naturally occurring antioxidants include retinol (vitamin A) and tocopherols (vitamin E), found in many animals and plants; ascorbic acid (vitamin C), found in citrus and other fruits and vegetables; and beta carotene, found in deep orange and dark green vegetables. These may play a significant role in the prevention of cancer, heart disease, immune-deficiency diseases, and aging.
Synthetic antioxidants include butylated hydroxy toluene (BHT), butylated hydroxy anisole (BHA), and propyl gallate. Natural and synthetic antioxidants are added to food to prevent undesirable deterioration. Foods preserved with antioxidants include vegetable oils, bread, and cheese.
Antioxidants are frequently applied to the packaging materials of cereals and nuts.

## Passage
Oxidation number is defined as the charge which an atom of the element has in its ion or appears to have when present in the combined state with other atoms.
There are certain rules for assigning oxidation number. Some of these rules are given below.
- The oxidation number of all the atoms of different elements in their respective elementary states and allotropic forms is taken to be zero.
- The O.N. of a monoatomic ion is the same as the charge on it.
- The O.N. of hydrogen is +1 when combined with nonmetals and is -1 when combined with active metals called metal hydrides
- The O.N. of alkali metals (Li, Na, K, etc.) is always + 1 and that of alkaline earth metals ( $\mathrm{Be}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Ca}$, etc.) is +2 .
- The algebraic sum of the O.N. of all the atoms in a compound must be zero. In polyatomic ion, the algebraic
sum of all the O.N.'s of atoms of the ion must equal the charge on the ion.

11. (a) Oxidation is a reaction which involves addition or gain of oxygen. For example,
$4 \mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \quad 2 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} 2 \mathrm{CuO}$
(b) Reduction is a reaction which involves loss or removal of oxygen, For example,
$\mathrm{CuO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \quad \mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{CO}$
12. Oxidation or reduction cannot take place alone. This is because if one substance loses oxygen, i.e., undergoes reduction, there must be another substance to take up this oxygen, i.e., undergo oxidation. Similarly, if one substance loses hydrogen, i.e., undergoes oxidation, there must be another substance to take up this hydrogen, i.e., undergo reduction. Hence, oxidation and reduction always take place together. That is why, these reactions are called redox reactions.

## Long Answer Questions
1. There are three different types of combination reactions:
(a) Combination of an element with another element to form a compound.
$2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(b) Combination of two or more compounds to form a new compound.
$$
\mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})
$$
(c) The combination of an element and a compound to form a new compound.
$$
2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
2. The four ions involved are $\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}, \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$. The combinations of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$with $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$and $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ with $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$ produce the compounds NaCl and $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$. If both of these compounds are soluble, no reaction occurs. In this case, however, $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is insoluble. Thus a reaction occurs which we can illustrate with a balanced chemical equation.
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})$
The equation written in total ionic form is
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) & +\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
& \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}
$$
The net ionic equation will be
$\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$

5. (a) Decomposition reaction : Carbohydrates are broken down to form glucose. Complex molecule broken down into simpler molecule during the digestion of food. Therefore it is a decomposition reaction.
(b) Redox reaction
Oxidation : $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
Reduction : $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Overall : $2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) +2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(c) Displacement reaction
$3 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{Al} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{Mn}+2 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
Aluminium being higher in reactivity series displaces Mn from its compound. Therefore it is an example of displacement reaction.
(d) Displacement reaction
$\mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Cu}$
Iron is placed at higher position than copper in reactivity series. Therefore iron displace copper from copper sulphate solution which result in the formation of $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ leaving behind copper.
(e) Neutralisation reaction
$\mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
NaOH which is a base react with hydrochloric acid leads to formation of sodium chloride (salt) and water. This is an example of neutralisation reaction.

3. Iron is oxidized in presence of atmospheric oxygen and water to form iron oxide trihydrate $\left(\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$, known as rust. It peels off iron surface exposing fresh iron surface for further rusting. While, aluminium, oxidized in presence of atmospheric oxygen to form a thin, transparent and non water-soluble layer of aluminium oxide $\left(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$, which shield the metal from further oxidation and maintains its metallic shine. Hence, the oxidation of iron to rust is a problem for structural engineers but the oxidation of aluminium is not.

Smell of sulphur is due to the formation of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ gases.

5. First, represent the name of the species involved as reactants and products with formulae and indicate their physical states.
$$
\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$
Next, balance carbon. Place a coefficient of 3 in front of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ to balance the carbons in $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}$. Balance the eight hydrogens in $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}$ by adding a coefficient of 4 in front of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
$\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Now note that there are 10 oxygens on the right. On the left, one oxygen is in $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}$ so nine are needed from $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. To get an odd number of oxygens from $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ we need to use a fractional coefficient, in this case $\frac{9}{2}$ (i.e., $\frac{9}{2} \times 2=9$ )
$$
\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\frac{9}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$
Finally, we need to clear the fraction so that all coefficients are whole numbers. Multiply the whole equation by 2 and do a quick check.
$$
2 \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+9 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
$$
6. (a) Because aluminum oxide is the oxide of a metal, we would expect it to be an ionic solid. Indeed it is, and it has a very high melting point, $2072^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(b) In its compounds, aluminum has a $3+$ charge, $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$, the oxide ion is $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$, consequently, the formula of aluminum oxide is $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. Metal oxides tend to be basic and therefore reacts with acids to form salts and water. In this case the salt formed is aluminum nitrate, $\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{3}$. The balanced chemical equation is
$\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+6 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$

Exothermic reaction : - Those reactions which occur with the evolution of heat are exothermic reactions. e.g. $2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \xrightarrow[\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\text { heat }]{ }$
Endothermic reaction : - Those reactions which occur with the absorption of heat are endothermic reactions. e.g.
$2 \mathrm{~Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} 2 \mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{s})+4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
10. Respiration is the most important biochemical reaction which releases energy in the cell. When we breathe air (oxygen) enters into our lungs and oxygen binds itself to haemoglobin present in red blood cells (RBC). It is then carried to million of cells present in our body. Respiration occurs in these cells and is accompanied by the combustion of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)$ producing carbon dioxide ( $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ) and water ( $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ). A large amount of energy is released in this chemical reaction.
$$
6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+2820 \mathrm{KJ}
$$
Since the process is accompanied by release of energy so it is considered as an exothermic reaction.

$2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$ (Decomposition reaction)

$\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})$
Double displacement reaction : - In this reaction exchange of ions between two reactants takes place. e.g.
$\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})$

- Acidic solution = pH less than 7.

- Changes blue litmus to red

- Metal carbonate/metal hydrogen carbonate + acid → salt $+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Base + acid → salt $+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Metal oxide + acid → salt + $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
- Conduct electricity due to presence of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion
- Hydrogen ion of acids cannot exist alone, they exist after combining with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecules.

## ACIDS
The word "acid" comes from the Latin word acidus, meaning "sour" or "tart," since aqueous solutions of acids have a sour or tart taste. Lemon and orange taste sour because they contain citric acid and ascorbic acid (vitamin C). Another common acid is vinegar, which is the sour liquid produced when apple cider, grape juice, or other plant juices ferment beyond the formation of alcohol. Vinegar is a 5 percent aqueous solution of acetic acid.
Liebig defined acid as a compound containing one or more replaceable hydrogen(s). Acid dissociate in water and produce hydrogen ion $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$e.g. HCl is an acid and on dissolving in water it gives $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions.
$$
\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}
$$
Hydrogen ions cannot exist alone but they exist after combining with water molecules. Thus hydrogen ion must always be shown as $\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})$ or hydronium ion $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right)$.
$$
\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}
$$
On the basis of sources acids can be classified into two groups. These are
(a) Organic acid: Acid obtained from plants material and animals is called organic acid. e.g citric acid (oranges \& lemons), acetic acid (vinegar), oleic acid (olive oil) etc.
(b) Mineral acid: Acid obtained from minerals is called mineral or inorganic acid. They do not contain carbon. e.g. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ etc.
## Physical Properties of Acids
(a) Taste : Acids are generally sour in taste.
(b) Effect on indicator : Acid changes the colour of indicators as given in the table.
| Indicator | Change of colour | |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Litmus | From | To |
| Methyl orange | Blue | Red |
| Phenolphthalein | Orange | Red |
(c) Electrical Conductivity : Acids conduct electricity in aqueous solution, since they contain dissolved ions. For example, soluble acids like hydrochloric acid and sulphuric acid are used as electrolytes.
(d) Corrosive action : Strong acids are corrosive in nature. They corrode metals and release hydrogen gas. They can also burn skin. Know!
Acids are generally not corrosive except mineral acids $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, \mathrm{HNO}{ }_{3}, \mathrm{HCl}\right.$ etc) and most of them are solids like organic acids (oxalic acid, tartaric acid, ascorbic acid, citric acid etc.)
## Chemical Properties of Acids
(a) Reaction on metals :
(i) Metals which lie above hydrogen in the metal activity series (already studied in chapter 1) generally react with dilute acids to form their respective salt and hydrogen.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Metal + Acid } \longrightarrow \text { Salt + Hydrogen } \\
& \begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s}) & +\underset{\text { Magnesi }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})}
\end{aligned} \longrightarrow \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Magnesium }
\end{array}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow} \\
& \begin{array}{c}
\text { Magnesium chloride } \\
\text { Hydrogen }(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{array} \longrightarrow \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})++\underset{\text { Iron }(\text { II }) \text { sulphate }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow} \\
\text { Iron }
\end{array} \text { Sulpdrogen }
\end{aligned}
$$
(ii) Highly active metals like potassium, sodium and calcium also react in similar manner, but they tend to explode on combining with acids.
$$
\underset{\text { Sodium }}{2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Dilute hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
Nitric acid (of various concentrations) usually exhibits oxidizing property, rather than acidic property. Metals such as magnesium combine with extremely dilute (1\%) nitric acid to liberate hydrogen.
$$
\underset{\text { Magnesium }}{\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Magnesium nitrate }}{\mathrm{Mg}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen }}{\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
Carbonic acid being very weak acid does not react with any metal at all.
## SOLUTION :
The gas liberated is hydrogen.
$$
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow
$$
Take a burning splinter near the gas. If it burns explosively with a 'pop' sound, the gas is hydrogen.

Granulated zinc is taken in a Woulfe's bottle. Dilute sulphuric acid is poured dropwise through a thistle funnel on granulated zinc pieces. The gas evolved is collected over water by the downward displacement of water.

- Make sure your experimental apparatus is set up properly.
- Sometimes the bubbles of hydrogen formed stick to the surface of zinc metal and this prevents further reaction of the acid on the metal. This can be prevented by adding a few crystals of copper sulphate to the reaction mixture.
Laboratory preparation of dihydrogen
(b) Reaction with basic oxides
Basic oxides get neutralized when they react with acids.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \\
& \underset{\text { Sodium oxide }}{\mathrm{Na} 2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
& \underset{\text { Lead oxide }}{\mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\mathrm{Nitric}_{2} \text { acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Lead nitrate }}{\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}} \\
& \underset{\text { Copper oxide }}{\mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Copper sulphate }}{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Acids undergo neutralization reaction with basic hydroxides to form salt and water.

(d) Reaction with carbonates and bicarbonates (hydrogen carbonates)
Acids react with carbonates and bicarbonates to form their respective salt, water and carbon dioxide.
Carbonate/Bicarbonate + Acid ⟶ Salt + Water + Carbon dioxide
$$
\underset{\text { Sodium carbonate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{\text { Zinc carbonate }}{\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Zinc sulphate }}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow} \\
& \underset{\text { Sod. hydrogen carbonate }}{2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium sulphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
\end{aligned}
$$
## SOLUTION :
The compound is calcium carbonate. The gas evolved is carbon dioxide which extinguishes a burning candle.
$$
\underset{\text { Calcium carbonate }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{dil}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
## (e) Reaction with sulphites and bisulphites (hydrogen sulphites)
Acids react with sulphites and bisulphites to form their respective salt, water and sulphur dioxide.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Sulphite/Bisulphite }+ \text { Acid } \longrightarrow \text { Salt }+ \text { Water + Sulphur dioxide } \\
& \underset{\text { Sodium sulphite }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+} \underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium nitrate }}{2 \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}} \\
& \underset{\text { Zinc sulphite }}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+} \underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Zinc nitrate }}{\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}} \\
& \underset{\substack{\text { Potassium hydrogen } \\
\text { sulphite }}}{\mathrm{KHSO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Potassium chloride }}{\mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
\end{aligned}
$$

CASE II : To determine whether acid will show their acidic behaviourcas in absence of water.
Take sodium chloride in a boiling tube and add some concentrated sulphuric acid to it. Following reaction will take place,
$$
2 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$
The gaseous hydrochloric acid formed, starts coming out of the tube.
Now, if we will place 'dry' blue litmus paper near the mouth of the tube it does not show any change, but if we place 'moist' blue litmus paper it turns red. This is because acids produces $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion only in presence of water, hence in absence of water they do not show acidic behaviour.

## CLASSIFICATION OF ACIDS
## (1) Classification Based on the Presence of Oxygen
(i) Oxy-acids
Oxy-acids are those, which contain oxygen in their composition.
Examples : Sulphuric acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)$, Oxalic acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)$, Nitric acid $\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right)$, Acetic acid $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$.
(ii) Hydracids
Acids which contain hydrogen together with other element and not any oxygen in their composition, are called hydracids. Examples: Hydrochloric and (HCl), Hydroiodic acid (HI), Hydrobromic acid (HBr).
Time to
Check Your Knowledge
## $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ is a hydracid or oxyacid?
## (2) Classification Based on the Strength of the Acid
Almost all the acids dissociate in water and produce hydrogen ion to a lesser or greater extent. The strength of an acid depends on the concentration of the hydrogen ions present in a solution. Greater the tendency to loose hydrogen ion in the solution, greater is the strength of acid. However, some acids do not dissociate to an appreciable extent in water such as carbonic acid. Therefore, these acids will have a low concentration of hydrogen ions present in a solution. Based on this, acids are classified as :
(i) Strong acids
An acid, which dissociates completely or almost completely in water, is classified as a strong acid.
Examples : Hydrochloric acid, sulphuric acid, nitric acid etc.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}
$$
Note: It must be noted that in these acids all the hydrogen ions combine with water molecule and exist as hydronium ions, $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$.
$$
\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}
$$
## (ii) Weak acids
An acid which dissociates negligibly or almost incompletely in water, is classified as a weak acid.
Examples : Acetic acid, formic acid, carbonic acid etc.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Acetic acid } & \\
\mathrm{HCOOH}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HCOO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Formic acid } & \\
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Carbonic acid } &
\end{aligned}
$$
Or
$$
\underset{\text { Carbonic acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
