As we have studied that acid on dissociation in water produce hydrogen ion, the number of these replaceable hydrogen ions in an acid is known as the basicity of an acid. It is also evident that, one hydrogen ion or hydronium ion can react with one hydroxyl ion ( $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$).
(i) Monobasic acid
A monobasic acid has one replaceable hydrogen ion. Hence, these acids combine with one hydroxyl group of a base to form salt and water.
Since, monobasic acids have only one replaceable hydrogen ion, they form only one kind of salt.
| Examples : | Hydrochloric acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| | Hydroiodic acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{I}^{-}$ |
| | Nitric acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$ |
| | Acetic acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$ |
| | Hypochlorous acid | $;$ | $\mathrm{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OCl}^{-}$ |
All the above acids have one replaceable hydrogen. Hence, they are monobasic acid and have basicity one.
(ii) Dibasic acid
A dibasic acid has two replaceable hydrogen ion. These acid combine with two hydroxyl groups of a base to form salt and water. It known as a dibasic acid. Such acids dissociate in two steps in water.
Example :
(i) $\quad \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
(ii) $\quad \mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})$
Since dibasic acid has two hydrogen ions, it can form two kinds of salts, i.e. the normal salt, and a hydrogen salt, as given below:
$$
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium hydrogen sulphate }}{\mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$
Example : $\quad$ Sulphuric acid $\quad ; \quad \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\text { Sulphurous acid } ; & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-} \\
\text { Carbonic acid } ; & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-} \\
\text { Oxalic acid } ; & (\mathrm{COOH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{COO}^{-} \\
\mathrm{COO}^{-}
\end{array}
$$
The tribasic acids can combine with three hydroxyl groups. These acids have three replaceable hydrogen ions. Example, phosphoric acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)$. Phosphoric acid dissociates in water in three steps:
(i) $\quad \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$
(ii) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$
(iii) $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$
Since, they are tribasic, therefore, they give three types of salts.
$$
\begin{array}{cc}
\underset{\text { Sodium Hydroxide }}{\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Monosodium dihydrogen phosphate }}{\mathrm{NaH}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} & +\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
\underset{\text { Sodium Hydroxide }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Disodium monohydrogen phosphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{HPO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} & \\
\underset{\text { Sodium Hydroxide }}{3 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\text { Trisodium phosphate }}}{\mathrm{Na}^{2}}+\underset{\text { Water }}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
\end{array}
$$
Note :- Acids containing more than one acidic hydrogen atom are called polyprotic acids.
Like acids, bases also dissociate in water, but in place of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$they produce hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. Thus, a base is a compound which on dissolving in water yields hydroxyl ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. The characteristic property of a base is due to the formation of these negative hydroxyl ions. A base may be an oxide or a hydroxide of a metal. If a base is soluble in water, it dissociates to form a metal ion and negative hydroxyl ion. Substances that combine with acids to form salt and water are also called bases.
Sodium hydroxide is a base, it gives sodium ion and hydroxyl ion when dissolved in water.
$$
\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
Ammonium hydroxide is also a base. In water, it dissociates to form ammonium ion and a hydroxyl ion.
$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
Alkalis are bases that dissociate in water to yield hydroxyl ion ( $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$). Sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, calcium hydroxide and ammonium hydroxide are the common alkalis.
$\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
$$
; \quad \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
$\mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{K}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
;
All alkalis are bases but not all bases are alkali. Alkali is a basic, ionic salt of an alkali or alkaline earth metal element. Alkalis are best known for being bases that dissolve in water. Whereas a base is most commonly thought of as a substance that can accept $\boldsymbol{H}+$ ions. A soluble base is also often referred to as an alkali if hydroxide ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$are involved.
Time to Check Your Knowledge
Whether calcium oxide $(\mathbf{C a O})$ is alkali, base or both?
It serves as both. As we know that alkalis are bases that dissociate in water to yield hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. The calcium oxide dissolves in water to form calcium hydroxide which further ionises to give hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$.
$$
\mathrm{CaO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}
$$
(a) Taste: Bases are bitter to taste. If you have ever gotten soap in your mouth, you have noticed its bitter taste. This bitter taste confirms presence of base in the soap.
(b) Feel: They are soapy and slippery to touch.
(c) Electrical conductivity: Some bases are good conductors of electricity. For example, soluble bases like sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are used as electrolytes.
(d) Corrosive action : Strong alkalis like sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are highly corrosive or caustic in nature. Sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are commonly called caustic soda and caustic potash respectively. Organic tissues like skin, etc. get completely corroded by these two alkalis. However, the other alkalis are only mildly corrosive.
(e) Effect on indicator: Bases changes the colour of indicator as given in the table.
| Indicator | Change of colour | |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Litmus | From | To |
| Methyl orange | Red | Blue |
| Phenolphthalein | Orange | Yellow |
| Turmeric | Colourless | Pink |
(a) Reaction with air
When exposed to air, alkalis combine with the carbon dioxide of the air, to form the respective carbonate.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium carbonate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Potassium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Potassium carbonate }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
\end{aligned}
$$
An application of an alkali reacting with carbon dioxide is the drying of lime i.e. white washing. The white washing of walls is due to the action of carbon dioxide of the air on calcium hydroxide. When milk of lime, a watery paste of calcium hydroxide, is applied on a wall, it reacts with the carbon dioxide of the air to form a white layer of calcium carbonate.
$$
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium carbonate }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$
Alkalis combine with ammonium salts to liberate ammonia gas.
Alkali + Ammonium salt ⟶ Salt + Water + Ammonia
$$
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Ammonium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium chloride }}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
$$
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Ammonium sulphate }}{\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium sulphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
- What will happen if we put aqueous solution of KOH and $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in a thick glass tube and connect this tube with other glass vessel containing Nessler's reagent (as shown in figure below.)?
Aqueous solution of KOH and $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ combines to form potassium sulphate with evolution of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
$$
2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Potassiumsulphate }}{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\underset{\text { Ammonia }}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})}
$$
This evolved ammonia combines with Nessler's reagent (alkaline solution of $\mathrm{K}_{2}\left[\mathrm{HgI}_{4}\right]$ ) and gives brown precipitate.
$$
2 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{HgI}_{4}+\mathrm{NH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{KOH} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Brown precipitate }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{NHgOHgI}}+7 \mathrm{KI}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
Bases combine with acids to form respective salt and water only.
$$
\begin{gathered}
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{3 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Phosphoric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Trisodium phosphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Acetic acid }}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium acetate }}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
\end{gathered}
$$
A neutralization reaction is the reaction in which an acid and a base react to form salt and water. It involves the combination of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion to generate water. These reactions are known as neutralisation reaction.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Acid }+ \text { Base } \text { ⟶ Salt }+ \text { Water } \\
& \mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
Example :
Acid and base in the above reaction will ionize in the following way,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{HCl} & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
\mathrm{NaOH} & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\mathrm{H}^{+}$of acid and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$of base will combine to form water $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ and remaining ions will combine to form respective salt.
Non-metallic oxides also react in same manner, hence non-metallic oxides are acidic in nature.
$$
2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
(d) Reaction with metal
Metals react with base with evolution of hydrogen gas.
$$
2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium zincate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{ZnO}_{2}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \uparrow
$$
Bases can be classified in various ways, depending on the following factors:
We have already studied that bases on dissociation produce hydroxyl ion ( $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$). The strength of a base depends on the concentration of the hydroxyl ion when it is dissolved in water. Based on this, bases are classified as,
(i) Strong base
A base that dissociates completely or almost completely in water is classified as a strong base. In other words, greater the tendency to loose hydroxyl ion in the solution, the stronger is the base.
Example: Sodium hydroxide : NaOH
Potassium hydroxide : KOH
Calcium hydroxide : $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
(ii) Weak base
A base that partially dissociates in water is classified as a weak base.
Example: Magnesium hydroxide : $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
Ammonium hydroxide : $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$
CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC
$\mathrm{Sr}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ is a strong base as it ionises almost completely in water
Concentration of a basic solution depends upon the percentage of the base dissolved in water. Based on this fact, bases are classified as,
(i) Concentrated base : A base that has a relatively high percentage of the base in its aqueous solution is classified as a concentrated base. Example concentrated sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, ammonium hydroxide etc.
(ii) Dilute base: A base that has a relatively low percentage of the base dissolved in its aqueous solution, is classified as a dilute base. Example dilute sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, ammonium hydroxide etc.
The acidity of a base is equal to the number of hydroxyl ions it has. It is also the number of hydrogen ions with which a base can combine as one hydrogen ion combines with one hydroxyl ion.
Based on the acidity bases can be classified as,
(i) Monoacidic base
It is a base that has only one hydroxyl ion. It combines with only one hydrogen ion. Hence, its acidity will be one.
Example : Sodium hydroxide, Potassium hydroxide: KOH , Lithium hydroxide: LiOH , Ammonium hydroxide: $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$.
$$
\underset{\text { Sodium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$
It is a base that has two hydroxyl groups and combines with two hydrogen ions. Hence, its acidity will be two.
Example : Iron (II) hydroxide: $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Magnesium hydroxide: $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Copper hydroxide: $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Zinc hydroxide : $\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, Lead hydroxide: $\mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$.
$$
\underset{\text { Calcium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Calcium chloride }}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$
A base that has three hydroxyl ions and can combines with three hydrogen ions. Hence, its acidity will be three.
Example: Aluminium hydroxide
$$
\underset{\text { Aluminium hydroxide }}{\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{3 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Aluminium chloride }}{\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$
We know that acidity of a base is equal to number of hydroxyl ions. Thus, barium hydroxide $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ having two hydroxyl group is a diacidic base.
$$
\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
Did You Know!
The process of dissolving an acid or a base in water is a highly exothermic one. Care must be taken while mixing concentrated nitric acid or sulphuric acid with water. The acid must always be added slowly to water with constant stirring. If water is added to a concentrated acid, the heat generated may cause the mixture to splash out and cause burns. The glass container may also break due to excessive local heating. Mixing an acid or base with water results in decrease in the concentration of ions $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$per unit volume. Such a process is called dilution and the acid or the base is said to be diluted.
Learn More
- Natural indicators can be extracted from a wide variety offlowers, fruits, roots, leaves and other parts of plants, e.g. tea leaves, beet roots, rose petals of various colours, red cabbage leaves, turmeric, black berries, and delphinium flowers. Litmus (a natural dye) is one of the most common and naturally available indicator. It is extracted from lichens (combination of algae and fungus). When this litmus is added to an acidic solution, it turns red and when added to a basic solution, it turns blue. It possess a purple colour in distilled water.
- Synthetic indicators are prepared from man made chemical substances like phenolphtalein, methyl orange etc.
Phenolphthalein is a synthetic indicator. It turns acidic solutions colorless and basic solutions pink. If the concentration of indicator is particularly strong, it can appear purple.
Methyl orange is also a synthetic indicator used more oftenly because of clear colour change. In a solution becoming less acidic, methyl orange moves from red to orange and finally to yellow with the reverse occuring for a solution increasing in acidity.
Depending upon the change in properties indicators are classified as:
- Halochromic indicator show change in color with respect to the change in medium, e.g: litmus, phenolphtalein, methyl orange etc. These indicators are generally used as acid-base indicators.
- Olfactory indicator causes change in odour/smell with respect to the change in medium. e.g: clove oil, onion, vanilla extract etc.
- Gustatory indicator causes change in taste with respect to the change in medium. e.g: Sour taste of milk indicates that the milk has became acidic, hence it is spoiled.
Universal indicators : It is a mixture of indicators often sold ready made solution which can indicate pH values, usually over a range of 3-11, by successive changes of colour
Take 100 mL beaker and fill it upto 80\% with distilled water in your school laboratory. First add few drops of phenolphthalein solution and stir solution with glass rod properly then record your observation if there is any change. Now add few drops of ammonium hydroxide and stir solution with glass rod properly then again record your observation if there is any change. Then add few drops of vinegar solution and stir resulting solution properly with glass rod. Record change if any. Now in case you are not able to understand the reason of your recorded observations, consult your friend and discuss with your teacher.

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1
examples.problem:
Write balanced chemical equations from the following word equations including the physical state of each element or compound.
(a) Sodium metal with water yields hydrogen gas and an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution.
(b) Potassium chlorate when heated yields potassium chloride and oxygen gas (ionic compounds are solids).
(c) An aqueous sodium chloride solution with an aqueous silver nitrate solution yields a silver chloride precipitate (solid) and a sodium nitrate solution.
(d) An aqueous phosphoric acid solution with an aqueous calcium hydroxide solution yields water and solid calcium phosphate.
examples.solution:
(a) 2 Na (s) $+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (l) $\longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}$ (g) +2 NaOH (aq)
(b) $2 \mathrm{KClO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(c) $\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})$
(d) $2 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Check whether the following represents the correct balanced chemical equation or not.
(a) $\quad \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CuO}+4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
Copper nitrate
(a) No, equation (a) is not correctly balanced, as it is not specified whether acid is dilute or concentrated. Moreover, state of reactants and products is also not mentioned. Thus, the correct balanced equation will be
$$
\underset{\text { Copper }}{\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { (Conc) }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Copper sulphate }}{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}
$$
(b) No, equation (b) is also not correctly balanced, as atoms of different elements are not equal in reactants and products. Moreover, state of reactants and products is not specified. Thus, the correct balanced equation will be
$$
\underset{\text { Copper nitrate }}{2 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Copper (II) oxide }}{2 \mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Nitrogen dioxide }}{4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}+\underset{\text { Oxygen }}{\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
You go through the concept of balancing of chemical equation thoroughly and ask your friend to do the same. Now divide your friends in two groups/and you also become part of one group. Convince your teacher to conduct a quiz in which your teacher will give you an equation. Students of both groups have to answer whether the given equation is balanced or not. If not then mention the parameter missing to balance the equation. The group which comes up with more number of correct identification will be the winner of quiz.
Note : If a group don't know the correct identification of the equation given to them then they can pass on that equation to other group for identification.
## CHEMICAL REACTION
The process in which two or more substances reacts to form new substances, is called chemical reactions.
It involves making and breaking of bond between atoms to form new a substance, which describes the nature and specific characteristic of the reaction.
## Types of Chemical Reactions
Based on the specific characteristics and the nature of the reactions, chemical reactions have been divided into following types :
## 1. Synthesis or Combination Reaction
When two or more elements or substances combine together to form a new substance, the chemical reaction is known as synthesis or combination reaction. This type of reaction takes place in presence of heat, light or may require electricity.
Examples :
$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeO} \\
2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{gathered}
$$
Combination reactions have wide applications in manufacturing of some industrially important compounds.
(1) At a commercial scale, ammonia is manufactured by Haber's process.
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g)
$$
(2) Ostwald process for manufacturing of nitric acid.
$$
4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(a q)
$$
(3) Contact process for manufacturing of sulphuric acid involves multiple stages of combination reactions.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l}) \\
& \begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
(\mathrm{Conc})
\end{array} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}(\mathrm{l}) \\
& \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l})
\end{aligned}
$$
(4) Manufacturing of ethyl alcohol by acid catalysed hydration of ethylene.
$$
\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{l})
$$
## Combination reactions can occur between following types of reactants:
(a) Combination reaction between two elements.
(a) Combination between two non-metals: When a mixture of hydrogen and chlorine is placed in sunlight, hydrogen chloride gas is formed.
$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Sunlight }} 2 \mathrm{HCl}
$$
(b) Combination between a metal and a non-metal : When magnesium wire is burnt in air, magnesium oxide is formed.
$$
2 \mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} 2 \mathrm{MgO}
$$
(b) Combination reaction between two compounds.
These are the reactions in which two compounds directly combine to form a new compound.
(a) Calcium oxide directly combines with water to form calcium hydroxide.
$$
\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}
$$
(b) Calcium oxide directly combines with carbon dioxide gas to form calcium carbonate.
$$
\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}
$$
(c) Formation of sulphuric acid from combination of sulphur trioxide and water which is a major contributor to nasty and toxic acid rain.
$$
\mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
(c) Combination reaction between a compound and an element.
In these reaction in which an element reacts with a compound to form a new substance.
Combination of oxygen with carbon monoxide produces carbondioxide gas.
$$
2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 2
examples.problem: Write balanced equations that predict the reactions of cesium metal with (a) $\mathbf{C l}_{\mathbf{2}}(\mathbf{g})$; and (b) $\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{2}}(\mathbf{g})$.
examples.solution:
The reaction of Cs with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ are simple combination reactions between two elements, one metal and one nonmetal, forming the compound CsH and CsCl ,
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{Cs}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CsCl}(\mathrm{~s}) \\
2 \mathrm{Cs}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CsH}(\mathrm{~s})
\end{aligned}
$$

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3
examples.problem: Ammonium nitrate, a colorless ionic solid used as a fertilizer, explodes when it is heated above $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The products are three gases: molecular nitrogen, molecular oxygen, and steam (water vapour). Find the type of chemical reaction and write a balanced equation for the explosion of ammonium nitrate.
examples.solution:
The description in the problem tells us what happens to the starting material: $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ breaks apart into molecules of $\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. An unbalanced form of the equation can be written from this description. Then we must balance each element in turn by inspection.
$$
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$
## Step 1
First, count all atoms of each element on each side of the equation to see whether the elements are balanced:
$$
2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}+2 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O}
$$
## Step 2
As we can see that nitrogen is already balanced. To balance the hydrogen atoms, we change the stoichiometric coefficient of water from 1 to 2 :
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+4 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
## Step 3
This step balances nitrogen and hydrogen, but oxygen is not yet balanced. Oxygen must be balanced without changing the 1 : 1 ratio of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ to $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and the $1: 2$ ratio of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. These ratios must be retained to keep hydrogen and nitrogen in balance. Thus the only coefficient that can be adjusted to balance the oxygen atoms is that of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. Two oxygen atoms are already present as in water and thus the remaining oxygen atom can be adjusted. by changing the coefficient of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ to $1 / 2$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}+4 \mathrm{H}+3 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
This balances the equation, but it is unrealistic from a molecular perspective because there is no such thing as half a molecule. To get rid of the $1 / 2$ without unbalancing the reaction, we multiply all the coefficients by 2 .
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
4 \mathrm{~N}+8 \mathrm{H}+6 \mathrm{O} & \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{~N}+8 \mathrm{H}+6 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now the reaction is balanced and reasonable from a molecular perspective.
Copper nitrate $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]$ gives positive ring test of nitrate ion ( $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$), but after heating small amount of copper nitrate in dry boiling tube over the burner flame, it does not give positive ring test any more because it undergoes thermal decomposition reaction.
$$
2 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{~s})+4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 4
examples.problem:
Identify the substance that is oxidised and the substance that is reduced in the following reactions.
(a) $4 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$
(b) $\mathrm{CuO(s)}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
examples.solution:
(a) $4 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$
Sodium metal is oxidised as it is gaining oxygen.
(b) $\mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Copper oxide is getting reduced as it is losing oxygen.

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 5
examples.problem: When aluminium metal is exposed to air, a protective layer of aluminium oxide ( $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) forms on its surface. The layer prevents further reaction between aluminium and oxygen, and it is the reason that aluminium beverage cans do not corrode. [In the case of iron, the rust, or iron (III) oxide, is too porous to protect the iron metal underneath, so rusting continues]. Write a balanced equation for the formation of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.
examples.solution:
The unbalanced equation is :
$$
\mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
We see that both Al and O appear only once on each side of the equation but in unequal numbers. To balance the Al atoms, we place 2 in front of Al :
$$
2 \mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
There are two O atoms on the left-hand side and three O atoms on the right-hand side of the equation. This inequality of O atoms can be eliminated by writing $\frac{3}{2}$ in front of the $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ on the left-hand side of the equation.
$$
2 \mathrm{Al}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
Now multiply the entire equation by 2 to convert $3 / 2$ to 3 .
$$
\text { i.e. } 4 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}
$$
The above equation is the balanced equation for the formation of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.
Place a small silver article (made up of pure silver) in open air for few days. Then record your observation. In case if there is any sort of change, then try to find out the exact reason for this change by discussing with your friends and teacher.
## RANCIDITY
The most important cause of deterioration in fats and fatty foods is oxidation. Oxidation of fats results in the replacement of an oxygen ion from a hydrogen ion in the fatty acid molecule. This substitution destabilizes the molecule and makes it possible for other odd chemical fragments to find a place along the chain. An unpleasant change in the flavor and odour of a food is develop, called rancidity. Unsaturated fats are more susceptible to oxidation than the saturated fats.
Factors which accelerate fat oxidation include trace metals (iron, zinc, etc.), salt, light, water, bacteria, and molds. Fat oxidation can be retarded by use of antioxidants (Antioxidants are compounds that inhibit chemical reactions with oxygen such as BHT, BHA, vitamin E, and vitamin C), by use of spices such as sage and rosemary, and by use of light and/or air tight wrapping.
Some high fat foods such as potato chips are packed in materials that protect them from light and oxygen and the containers are flooded with nitrogen to further exclude oxygen. The nitrogen also serves as a cushion to minimize breakage of the chips during transport.
## Learn
## More
Oxidation reactions may involve highly reactive molecules called free radicals. Free radicals are molecules that have lost an electron and try to gain it by reacting with other molecules. This causes the substance to break down. Metals often catalyze reactions with oxygen. Another example would be the half eaten apple. The skinless area quickly oxidized causing the apple to turn brown. Antioxidants inhibit these changes by reacting with the free radicals before they can react with oxygen (free radical scavenging) or by reacting with the metals. Naturally occurring antioxidants may also work by interacting directly with oxygen.
Naturally occurring antioxidants include retinol (vitamin A) and tocopherols (vitamin E), found in many animals and plants; ascorbic acid (vitamin C), found in citrus and other fruits and vegetables; and beta carotene, found in deep orange and dark green vegetables. These may play a significant role in the prevention of cancer, heart disease, immune-deficiency diseases, and aging.
Synthetic antioxidants include butylated hydroxy toluene (BHT), butylated hydroxy anisole (BHA), and propyl gallate. Natural and synthetic antioxidants are added to food to prevent undesirable deterioration. Foods preserved with antioxidants include vegetable oils, bread, and cheese.
Antioxidants are frequently applied to the packaging materials of cereals and nuts.

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1
examples.problem: Which gas is usually liberated when an acid reacts with a metal? Illustrate with an example. How will you test for the presence of this gas ?
examples.solution:
The gas liberated is hydrogen.
$$
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow
$$
Take a burning splinter near the gas. If it burns explosively with a 'pop' sound, the gas is hydrogen.
Ask your teacher to demonstrate lab preparation of hydrogen in your school lab. Set up the experimental set as shown in following figure.
Granulated zinc is taken in a Woulfe's bottle. Dilute sulphuric acid is poured dropwise through a thistle funnel on granulated zinc pieces. The gas evolved is collected over water by the downward displacement of water.
In the same experiment if dil $\mathrm{NHO}_{3}$ or dil HCl is used instead of dil $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ What will happen? do you find the same observation?
## Precautionary measures
- Make sure your experimental apparatus is set up properly.
- Sometimes the bubbles of hydrogen formed stick to the surface of zinc metal and this prevents further reaction of the acid on the metal. This can be prevented by adding a few crystals of copper sulphate to the reaction mixture.
Laboratory preparation of dihydrogen
(b) Reaction with basic oxides
Basic oxides get neutralized when they react with acids.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \\
& \underset{\text { Sodium oxide }}{\mathrm{Na} 2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})} \\
& \underset{\text { Lead oxide }}{\mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\mathrm{Nitric}_{2} \text { acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Lead nitrate }}{\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}} \\
& \underset{\text { Copper oxide }}{\mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Copper sulphate }}{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}}
\end{aligned}
$$
(c) Reaction with basic hydroxides
Acids undergo neutralization reaction with basic hydroxides to form salt and water.
\[
\]
(d) Reaction with carbonates and bicarbonates (hydrogen carbonates)
Acids react with carbonates and bicarbonates to form their respective salt, water and carbon dioxide.
Carbonate/Bicarbonate + Acid ⟶ Salt + Water + Carbon dioxide
$$
\underset{\text { Sodium carbonate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium chloride }}{2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{\text { Zinc carbonate }}{\mathrm{ZnCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Zinc sulphate }}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow} \\
& \underset{\text { Sod. hydrogen carbonate }}{2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium sulphate }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Carbon dioxide }}{2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
\end{aligned}
$$

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 2
examples.problem: Metal compound A reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid to produce effervescence. The gas evolved extinguishes a burning candle. Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction, if one of the compounds formed is calcium chloride.
examples.solution:
The compound is calcium carbonate. The gas evolved is carbon dioxide which extinguishes a burning candle.
$$
\underset{\text { Calcium carbonate }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{dil}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
## (e) Reaction with sulphites and bisulphites (hydrogen sulphites)
Acids react with sulphites and bisulphites to form their respective salt, water and sulphur dioxide.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Sulphite/Bisulphite }+ \text { Acid } \longrightarrow \text { Salt }+ \text { Water + Sulphur dioxide } \\
& \underset{\text { Sodium sulphite }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+} \underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Sodium nitrate }}{2 \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}} \\
& \underset{\text { Zinc sulphite }}{\mathrm{ZnSO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+} \underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Zinc nitrate }}{\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}} \\
& \underset{\substack{\text { Potassium hydrogen } \\
\text { sulphite }}}{\mathrm{KHSO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Potassium chloride }}{\mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+}+\underset{\text { Sulphur dioxide }}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
\end{aligned}
$$
Time to
## Check Your Knowledge
- What will happen if lead sulphite is taken in a test tube and few $\mathbf{m L}$ of aqueous nitric acid is added to it. After that litmus paper is placed at the mouth of test tube?
Litmus paper will turn red as it is exposed to evolved gas sulphur dioxide which is acidic in nature. Reaction occur is following
$$
\underset{\text { Lead sulphite }}{\mathrm{PbSO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\underset{\text { Nitric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Lead nitrate }}{\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Water }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\substack{\text { Sulphurdioxide } \\ \text { (acidic) }}}{\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \uparrow}
$$
## (f) Reaction with metal sulphides
Acids react with metal sulphides to form their respective salt and hydrogen sulphide.
Metals sulphide + Acid ⟶ Salt + Hydrogen sulphide
$\underset{\text { Magnesium sulphide }}{\mathrm{MgS}(\mathrm{s})}+\underset{\text { Hydrochloric acid }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Magnesium chloride }}{\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen sulphide }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\underset{\text { Iron (II) sulphide }}{\mathrm{FeS}(\mathrm{s})}+\underset{\text { Sulphuric acid }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Iron (II) sulphate }}{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{\text { Hydrogen sulphide }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g})}$

examples.title: Check Your Knowledge
examples.problem:
Time to Check Your Knowledge
Whether calcium oxide $(\mathbf{C a O})$ is alkali, base or both?
examples.solution:
It serves as both. As we know that alkalis are bases that dissociate in water to yield hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$. The calcium oxide dissolves in water to form calcium hydroxide which further ionises to give hydroxyl ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$.
$$
\mathrm{CaO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}
$$

examples.title: Check Your Knowledge
examples.problem: ## - - Time to Check Your Knowledge - On the basis of acidity of bases, barium hydroxide is which type of base?
examples.solution:
We know that acidity of a base is equal to number of hydroxyl ions. Thus, barium hydroxide $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ having two hydroxyl group is a diacidic base.
$$
\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
Did You Know!

examples.title: Exercise 1 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q1
examples.problem:
Which of the following is a decomposition reaction?
(a) $2 \mathrm{HgO} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} 2 \mathrm{Hg}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(c) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\text { Electrolysis }} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(d) All of these
examples.solution: (d) A decomposition reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which a single compound breaks down into two or more elements or new compounds.

examples.title: Exercise 1 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q2
examples.problem:
On the basis of following features, identify the correct option.
(i) This reaction occurs during corrosion.
(ii) This reaction occurs during respiration.
(a) Decomposition reaction
(b) Redox reaction
(c) Combination reaction
(d) Endothermic reaction
examples.solution: (b) Both are redox reactions. Redox reactions are characterised by the transfer of electrons between chemical species. One species undergoes oxidation while another species undergoes reduction.

examples.title: Exercise 1 — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q3

(b) Melting of ice to give water.
(c) Dissolution of salt in water.

examples.solution: (d) Combustion of liquefied petroleum gas is a chemical change. As it is an irreversible reaction and new products (carbon dioxide and water vapours) are formed during the change. Also, a lot of heat is released during this reaction.

examples.solution: (c) O.N. of C is zero in $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q1
examples.problem: Write a balanced chemical equation when magnesium metal reacts with aqueous hydrochloric acid to produce a solution of magnesium chloride and hydrogen gas.
examples.solution: $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q2
examples.problem: Can a combination reaction be redox reaction?
examples.solution: Yes, a combination reaction can be a redox reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q3
examples.problem: What are the different types of reactions ?
examples.solution:
Different types of reaction are-
(i) Combination reactions
(ii) Decomposition reactions
(iii) Displacement reactions
(iv) Double decomposition reactions
(v) Redox reaction

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q4
examples.problem: What is a decomposition reaction ? Give example.
examples.solution:
The conversion of a single compound into two or more simpler substances is called as decomposition reaction. For example,
$$
\underset{\substack{\text { mercury(II) } \\ \text { oxide }}}{2 \mathrm{HgO}(\mathrm{~s})} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} \underset{\text { mercury }}{2 \mathrm{Hg}(\mathrm{l})}+\underset{\text { oxygen }}{\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}
$$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q5
examples.problem: Define displacement reaction.
examples.solution:
A reaction in which an atom or group of atoms present in a molecule is displaced by another atom is known as displacement reaction. For example,
$$
\mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
$$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q6
examples.problem: What happens when sodium reacts with water ?
examples.solution:
Sodium being more reactive than hydrogen, displaces hydrogen from water.
$$
2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q7
examples.problem: Write the chemical equation and name the reaction when a solution of sodium chloride is mixed with a solution of silver nitrate and a white precipitate of silver chloride is formed.
examples.solution:
$\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl} \downarrow+\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}$ (aq)
sodium chloride silver nitrate silver chloride sodium nitrate (white ppt.)
The reaction involves the exchange of radicals and is known as double displacement reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q8
examples.problem: Potassium chlorate $\left(\mathrm{KClO}_{3}\right)$ on heating forms potassium chloride and oxygen. Write a balanced equation for this reaction.
examples.solution: $2 \mathrm{KClO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KCl}(\mathrm{s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q9
examples.problem: Give an example of a chemical reaction characterised by the change in temperature.
examples.solution: The chemical reaction between quicklime and water to form slaked lime is characterised by the change in temperature (rise in temperature).

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q10
examples.problem: To balance a chemical equation, can we change the formulae of either reactants or products ?
examples.solution: No, we cannot change the chemical formulae of reactants or products to balance a chemical equation.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q11
examples.problem: Nickel(II) nitrate is prepared by heating nickel metal with liquid dinitrogen tetraoxide. In addition to the nitrate, gaseous nitrogen monoxide is formed. Write the balanced equation.
examples.solution: $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Very Short Answer Questions — Q12
examples.problem:
Write a balanced chemical equation with state symbols for the following reactions.
(a) Aquaeous solutions of barium chloride and sodium sulphate react to give insoluble barium sulphate and the solution of sodium chloride.
(b) Sodium hydroxide solution (in water) reacts with hydrochloric acid solution (in water) to produce sodium chloride solution and water.
examples.solution:
(a) $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}($ aq $)+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}($ aq $) \longrightarrow$
$$
\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s}) \downarrow+2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
(b) $\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q1
examples.problem: In the refining of silver, the recovery of silver from silver nitrate solution involved displacement by copper metal. Write down the reaction involved.
examples.solution:
$2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{eq})+2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})$
The displacement reaction occurs because copper is more reactive than silver. Shining white deposit of silver is formed on the copper metal and the solution becomes blue due to the formation of copper nitrate.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q2
examples.problem:
Translate the following statements into chemical equations and then balance them.
(a) Hydrogen gas combines with nitrogen to form ammonia.
(b) Hydrogen sulphide gas burns in air to give water and sulphur dioxide
(c) Barium chloride reacts with aluminum sulphate to give aluminum chloride and a precipitate of barium sulphate.
(d) Potassium metal reacts with water to give potassium hydroxide and hydrogen gas.
examples.solution:
(a) $3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
(b) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(c) $3 \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow$
$$
2 \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{BaSO}_{4} \downarrow
$$
(d) $2 \mathrm{~K}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q3
examples.problem: What is rancidity ?
examples.solution: When food material left for longer time, the fat and oil present in them undergoes oxidation process that results in change of taste and smell of the food and make it unfit for consumption. The process of such oxidation of fat and oil is known as rancidity.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q4
examples.problem:
Balance the following chemical reactions.
(a) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{10}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$
(c) $\mathrm{HIO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(d) $\mathrm{As}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AsCl}_{5}$
examples.solution:
(a) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{10}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$
(c) $2 \mathrm{HIO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(d) $2 \mathrm{As}+5 \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{AsCl}_{5}$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q5
examples.problem:
Balance the chemical equations for the following important industrial processes :
(a) Molecular nitrogen and hydrogen combine to give ammonia.
(b) Molecular hydrogen and carbon monoxide react to form methanol $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}\right)$.
(c) $\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{CaC}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
examples.solution:
(a) $\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(b) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
(c) $\mathrm{CaO}+3 \mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{CaC}_{2}$
(d) $2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q6
examples.problem:
Represent each of the following word reaction with a balanced chemical equation.
(a) Disilane gas $\left(\mathrm{Si}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}\right)$ undergoes combustion to form solid silicon dioxide and water.
(b) Solid aluminium hydride is formed by a combination reaction of its two elements.
(c) When solid calcium bisulfite is heated, it decomposes to solid calcium oxide, sulfur dioxide gas, and water.
examples.solution:
(a) $2 \mathrm{Si}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})+7 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{SiO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(b) $2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{AlH}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$
(c) $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HSO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q7
examples.problem: A large piece of zinc metal is placed in a copper (II) sulfate, $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$, solution. The blue solution become colourless as copper metal falls to the bottom of the container. The resulting solution contains zinc sulfate, $\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}$. Write balanced chemical equation for the reaction.
examples.solution:
In this displacement reaction, the more active metal, zinc, displaces the ions of the less active metal, copper, from the aqueous solution of copper sulphate.
The balanced chemical equation is
$\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q8
examples.problem: A solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is mixed with a solution of $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$. Write the molecular, total ionic and net ionic equations illustrating the reaction.
examples.solution:
$2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{KNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})$
$2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{S}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow$
$$
\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
$2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{S}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~s})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q9
examples.problem:
Write decomposition reactions for the following compounds.
(a) $\quad \mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
(b) $\quad \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
examples.solution:
(a) $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(b) $2 \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(c) $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q10
examples.problem: Show that oxidation and reduction reactions go side by side by taking a suitable example.
examples.solution:
$\mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{CO}$
If a substance gains oxygen during a reaction it is said to be oxidised and if a substance loses oxygen it is said to be reduced. In the above reaction carbon is oxidised to carbon monoxide, while zinc oxide is reduced to zinc. Thus, oxidation and reduction reactions are going side by side in the above reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q11
examples.problem:
Explain the following in terms of gain or loss of oxygen with two examples each.
(a) Oxidation
(b) Reduction
examples.solution:
(a) Oxidation is a reaction which involves addition or gain of oxygen. For example,
$4 \mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \quad 2 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} 2 \mathrm{CuO}$
(b) Reduction is a reaction which involves loss or removal of oxygen, For example,
$\mathrm{CuO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \quad \mathrm{ZnO}+\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{CO}$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q12
examples.problem: Can oxidation or reduction take place alone? Why or why not? What are such reactions called?
examples.solution: Oxidation or reduction cannot take place alone. This is because if one substance loses oxygen, i.e., undergoes reduction, there must be another substance to take up this oxygen, i.e., undergo oxidation. Similarly, if one substance loses hydrogen, i.e., undergoes oxidation, there must be another substance to take up this hydrogen, i.e., undergo reduction. Hence, oxidation and reduction always take place together. That is why, these reactions are called redox reactions.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q13
examples.problem:
(a) Solution of a substance ' X ' is used for testing carbon dioxide. Write the equation of the reaction of ' X ' with carbon dioxide.
(b) How is ' X ' obtained? Write chemical equation.
examples.solution:
(a) Solution of a substance X can be of calcium hydroxide.
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \underset{\text { (White ppt.) }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) ' X ' can be obtained by the reaction of calcium oxide with water.
$\mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+$ Heat

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q14
examples.problem: When hydrogen gas is passed over heated copper (II) oxide, copper and steam are formed. Write the balanced chemical equation with physical states for this reaction. State what kind of chemical reaction is this?
examples.solution:
$\mathrm{CuO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
The above chemical reaction is a redox reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q15
examples.problem:
What is observed when carbon dioxide gas is passed through lime water.
(a) For a short duration?
(b) For a long duration? Also write the chemical equations for the reaction involved.
examples.solution:
(a) Lime water turns milky due to the formation of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ which is insoluble in water.
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Lime water White ppt.
(b) A clear solution is obtained due to the formation of calcium bicarbonate $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ which is soluble in water.
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q16
examples.problem:
A student added few pieces of aluminium metal to two test tubes A and B containing aqueous solutions of iron sulphate and copper sulphate. In the second part of her experiment, she added iron metal to another test tubes C and D containing aqueous solution of aluminium sulphate and copper sulphate.
In which test tube or test tube will she observe colour change? On the basis of this experiment, state which one is the most reactive metal and why?
examples.solution:
A- $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}$
B $-\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ D $-\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$
$3 \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{Al} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}+3 \mathrm{Fe}$
(A)
$3 \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{Al} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}+\mathrm{Cu}$
(B)
$\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}+\mathrm{Fe} \longrightarrow$ No reaction
(C)
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Fe} \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Cu}$
(D)
Color change occurs in test tube A, B and D, no color change will be observed in test tube ( C ) becuase there is no reaction.
Al is most reactive because it displace copper and iron from their sulphate salts.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q17
examples.problem:
Identify the type of reactions taking place in each of the following cases and write the balanced chemical equation for the reactions.
(a) Zinc reacts with silver nitrate to produce zinc nitrate and silver.
(b) Potassium iodide reacts with lead nitrate to produce potassium nitrate and lead iodide.
examples.solution:
(a) $\mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{Ag}$
As zinc is more reactive than silver, therefore it displaces silver from its nitrate solution.
It is a displacement reaction.
(b) $2 \mathrm{KI}+\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{KNO}_{3}+\mathrm{PbI}_{2}$
(yellow)
A yellow precipitate of lead iodide is formed. It is a precipitation reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q18
examples.problem: 2 g of silver chloride is taken in a china dish and the china dish is placed in sunlight for sometime. What will be your observation in this case? Write the chemical reaction involved in the form of a balanced chemical equation. Identify the type of chemical reaction.
examples.solution:
When 2 g of AgCl taken in a china dish and placed in sunlight then it turns to black/grey metallic colured silver along with the liberation of chlorine gas.
The balanced chemical reaction will be-
$2 \mathrm{AgCl} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
This is a decomposition reaction. As the decomposition reaction is carried out by sunlight so it is called photolytic decomposition reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Short Answer Questions — Q19
examples.problem:
Mention with reason the colour changes observed when :
(i) silver chloride is exposed to sunlight.
(ii) copper powder is strongly heated in the presence of oxygen.
(iii) a piece of zinc is dropped in copper sulphate solution.
examples.solution:
(i) Photo decomposition of silver chloride takes place which results in formation of silver and colour changing to grey.
$$
2 \mathrm{AgCl}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}
$$
(ii) Copper undergoes oxidation and black coloured copper oxide is formed.
$$
2 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CuO}
$$
(iii) Zinc displaces copper from its solution and colour of the solution changes from blue to colourless.
$$
\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \text { (aq.) } \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4} \text { (aq.) }
$$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Long Answer Questions — Q1
examples.problem: What are different types of combination reactions ?
examples.solution:
There are three different types of combination reactions:
(a) Combination of an element with another element to form a compound.
$2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(b) Combination of two or more compounds to form a new compound.
$$
\mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})
$$
(c) The combination of an element and a compound to form a new compound.
$$
2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Long Answer Questions — Q2
examples.problem: A solution of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ is mixed with a solution of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$. Predict what happens.
examples.solution:
The four ions involved are $\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}, \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$. The combinations of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$with $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$and $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ with $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$ produce the compounds NaCl and $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$. If both of these compounds are soluble, no reaction occurs. In this case, however, $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is insoluble. Thus a reaction occurs which we can illustrate with a balanced chemical equation.
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})$
The equation written in total ionic form is
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) & +\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
& \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}
$$
The net ionic equation will be
$\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Long Answer Questions — Q3
examples.problem:
Balance the following equations
(a) $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(d) $\mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AlPO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(e) $\quad \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
examples.solution:
(a) $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(c) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(d) $2 \mathrm{Al}+2 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{AlPO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}$
(e) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Long Answer Questions — Q4
examples.problem:
Write balanced equations by predicting the products of the following reactions. Include the physical state of each element or compound.
(a) The neutralization of aqueous rubidium hydroxide with hydrobromic acid.
(b) A single-displacement reaction of calcium metal with a nitric acid solution.
(c) The combustion of solid naphthalene $\left(\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$.
(d) A precipitation reaction involving aqueous ammonium chromate and aqueous barium bromide.
(e) The decomposition of solid aluminum hydroxide into solid aluminum oxide and gaseous water.
examples.solution:
(a) $\mathrm{HBr}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{RbOH}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{RbBr}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(b) $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(c) $\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{H}_{8}(\mathrm{~s})+12 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 10 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(d) $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{BaBr}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow$
$$
\mathrm{BaCrO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Br}(\mathrm{aq})
$$
(e) $2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Long Answer Questions — Q5
examples.problem:
Identify the type of chemical reaction in the following statements and define each of them :
(a) Digestion of food in our body
(b) Rusting of iron
(c) Heating of manganese dioxide with aluminium powder
(d) Blue colour of copper sulphate solution disappears when iron fillings are added to it
(e) Dilute hydrochloric acid is added to sodium hydroxide solution to form sodium chloride and water.
examples.solution:
(a) Decomposition reaction : Carbohydrates are broken down to form glucose. Complex molecule broken down into simpler molecule during the digestion of food. Therefore it is a decomposition reaction.
(b) Redox reaction
Oxidation : $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
Reduction : $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Overall : $2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) +2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
(c) Displacement reaction
$3 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{Al} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{Mn}+2 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
Aluminium being higher in reactivity series displaces Mn from its compound. Therefore it is an example of displacement reaction.
(d) Displacement reaction
$\mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Cu}$
Iron is placed at higher position than copper in reactivity series. Therefore iron displace copper from copper sulphate solution which result in the formation of $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ leaving behind copper.
(e) Neutralisation reaction
$\mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
NaOH which is a base react with hydrochloric acid leads to formation of sodium chloride (salt) and water. This is an example of neutralisation reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q1
examples.problem: Why is photosynthesis considered as an endothermic reaction?
examples.solution: Photosynthesis is an endothermic reaction because light energy in the form of sun light is absorbed during the process by green plants.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q2
examples.problem: Why is the amount of gas collected in one of the test tubes in electrolysis of water double of the amount collected in the other? Name this gas.
examples.solution: It is because on electrolysis water decomposes to form hydrogen and oxygen in the ratio of $2: 1$ by volume. The gas with double volume is hydrogen.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q3
examples.problem: The oxidation of iron to rust is a problem, but the oxidation of aluminium to aluminium oxide is not why?
examples.solution: Iron is oxidized in presence of atmospheric oxygen and water to form iron oxide trihydrate $\left(\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$, known as rust. It peels off iron surface exposing fresh iron surface for further rusting. While, aluminium, oxidized in presence of atmospheric oxygen to form a thin, transparent and non water-soluble layer of aluminium oxide $\left(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$, which shield the metal from further oxidation and maintains its metallic shine. Hence, the oxidation of iron to rust is a problem for structural engineers but the oxidation of aluminium is not.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q4
examples.problem: Why the green colour of ferrous sulphate salt changes to brown and produces smell of burning sulphur on strong heating?
examples.solution:
Decomposition reaction
$\underset{\text { (green) }}{2 \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underset{\text { (brown) }}{\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})}$
Smell of sulphur is due to the formation of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ gases.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q5
examples.problem: Why colourless lead nitrate produces a yellow residue and brown fumes on heating?
examples.solution: $\underset{\text { (colourless) }}{2 \mathrm{~Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{~s})} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underset{\text { (yellow) }}{2 \mathrm{PbO}(\mathrm{s})}+\underset{\text { (brown fumes) }}{4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q6
examples.problem: When an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is added to common salt solution, then a white precipitate is formed. Why?
examples.solution:
$\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}(\mathrm{aq})$ white ppt.
Double displacement reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q7
examples.problem: Why the formation of black precipitate takes place, when hydrogen sulphide gas is passed through an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ ?
examples.solution:
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CuS}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})$ Black ppt.
Double displacement reaction.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q8
examples.problem: Why sodium metal reacts violently with water?
examples.solution: Reaction between sodium metal and water is highly exothermic. During the course of the reaction hydrogen gas produces which catches fire easily.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q9
examples.problem: Why antioxidants are added to fat and oil containing food?
examples.solution: When antioxidants are added to food, they act as reducing agent. Therefore the fat and oil present in the food do not get oxidised easily consequently prevent rancidity.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — Reasoning Based Questions — Q10
examples.problem: Why magnesium ribbon burns in air with a dazzling flame and forms white ash?
examples.solution: When magnesium ribbon burns in air, addition of oxygen to magnesium takes place to produce magnesium oxide. This process accompanied with the evolution of heat and light due to which dazzling flame observed.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — HOTS Questions — Q1
examples.problem: A substance X , which is an oxide of a group 2 element, is used intensively in the cement industry. This element is present in bones also. On treatment with water it forms a solution which turns red litmus blue. Identify $X$ and also write the chemical reactions involved.
examples.solution:
Substance calcium oxide.
$\mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ turns red litmus blue.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — HOTS Questions — Q2
examples.problem: A solution of KBr is mixed with a solution of $\mathrm{Sr}\left(\mathrm{ClO}_{4}\right)_{2}$. Predict what happens.
examples.solution: The four ions involved are $\mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}, \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-}$. An exchange of ions produces the compounds $\mathrm{KClO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{SrBr}_{2}$. Both of these compounds are soluble, so no reaction occurs.

examples.title: Assertion \& Reason — HOTS Questions — Q3
examples.problem:
Identify the reducing agent in the following reactions.
(a) $4 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(b) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{F}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HF}+\mathrm{HOF}$
(c) $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(d) $2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
examples.solution:
